sessions.description: Description: 10min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to warm up  6x3min Very Hard RPE8 (5k) pace, 3min easy  9min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to cool down

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Chest Sculling drills w/ 10s rest between drills 5x200m Moderate-Hard, RPE7 w/ 45s rest between intervals 4x50m Fastest Average, RPE9 w/ 20s rest between sprints 8x25m Side Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Chest Sculling (CS) - With your chest facing down, bend your elbows and point your fingertips down while keeping your elbows high. Make the same figure-eight patterns with both hands to propel yourself forward, effectively repeating the center of the stroke.  Side Kicking (SK) - Swim on your side with your lower arm extended ahead of you and the upper arm resting against your side. Rest your head against your shoulder and look down, keeping your head completely submerged while using only your kick to propel yourself. Rotate your head slightly when inhaling and try to keep your hips & legs high throughout as you swim a full pool length before switching sides.

TSS 96, IF 0.80, kJ(Cal) 654.  Description: Elbow consists of 4x8.5-minute segments ranging from 99-100% FTP, each separated by 8.5-minute recoveries. Goals: The goal here is to elevate your strength endurance by learning to ride for longer and longer durations at your current FTP. As your body grows accustomed to a certain level of stress, your ability to withstand that stress for longer periods of time improves. Before you know it, your muscles don't load up like they used to, your mind isn't completely consumed with the desire to ease up, and your so-called "hour power" gravitates toward truer hour-power.The recommended cadence range is wide, 85-105 rpm, and accommodates many styles of riding. Riders wishing to focus more on improvements in strength endurance will spin toward the lower end of this cadence range while riders looking to foster a quicker spin will ride toward the upper end of the range.Or mix and match from interval to interval, just do your best to keep the power output smooth and consistent over the course of these 4 intervals.

TSS 56, IF 0.87, kJ(Cal) 368.  Description: Frost is 2x10-minute intervals that range from 99-105% of FTP with 3-minutes of recovery in between. Goals: The primary objective here is improvement in muscular endurance accomplished by growing your ability to repeat efforts at high percentages of your FTP.By working close to but below your FTP, you not only accumulate a lot of time at a meaningful level of stress, but recovery from slightly sub-threshold effort comes much more readily than recovery following work done at or just above your FTP.Try to hold your spin above 85rpm and even as high as 105rpm depending on your sustainable power and cadence goals.

TSS 54, IF 0.85, kJ(Cal) 364.  Description: Joe Devel -4 scales things down a bit but still consists of 4 sets of Anaerobic power repeats in various shapes and sizes, all consisting of microbursts of different work durations but the same 15-second recovery valleys.Set 1 consists of 15-second On-segments at 150% FTP, set 2 consists of 30-second On-segments at 130% FTP, set 3 consists of 45-second On-segments at 120% FTP and set 4 consists of 18-second On-segments at 140% FTP.All recoveries between sets are 5 minutes long. Goals: As with any power repeat workout, the aim is to exploit your work capacity rather than build it. So while workouts with plenty of recovery between intervals help you BUILD your ability to do a lot of work, workouts like Joe Devel place the intervals close together and force you to actually DO a lot of work. All told, you'll have the opportunity to accumulate a lot of time working heavily anaerobically, relying on your sugar stores as much or more than your oxygen intake.But don't let that fool you into believing your aerobic system won't be operating full-tilt, because it will since these short recoveries don't allow your respiration to drop far & make each subsequent interval take less and less time to push you to your aerobic capacity, aka VO2max.All of this fancy-talk is just to say you're gonna push your limits in a number of productive ways. Keep your spin quick, upwards of 100rpm, but stay controlled & relaxed - no bouncing, no slop.

TSS 28, IF 0.53, kJ(Cal) 350.  Description: Obelisk is 4x3-minute intervals of leg-speed drills at a very low 60% FTP with 3 minutes of rest between intervals. Keep the pressure on the pedals light and your intensity low to moderate regardless of your cadence.  Goals: Leg-speed drills are aimed at improving pedal economy during a light/recovery ride. Focus on actively kicking over the top of the pedal stroke & pulling back across the bottom of the pedal stroke while remaining relaxed all with the intent of refining your pedaling technique, expending less energy at higher cadences & accumulating some extra, low-intensity mileage. For the 3-minute intervals, the beginning cadence should be above 90rpm, then simply spin more quickly until you?ve reached a spin between 110-120rpm without rocking or bouncing in the saddle. Remain smooth & relaxed and hold this high rpm for the duration of the interval as long as you can maintain your form.

TSS 18, IF 0.49, kJ(Cal) 240.  Description: Lazy Mountain -2 is 45 minutes of easy, steady effort between 45-55% FTP. Goals: Recovery rides can be used to further your aerobic base by riding very easily when your muscles are already pretty cooked. When recovery is the goal, keep the ride as short as necessary and pull the plug well inside of an hour if effort this low is wearing you down and postponing your recovery.Spin lightly and reasonably quickly, preferably above 85rpm.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Bilateral Breathing drills w/ 10s rest between drills 6x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals 5x100m Hard, RPE8 w/ 60s rest between intervals 8x25m Kick & Rotate drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Bilateral Breathing (BB) - Swim your regular freestyle stroke while breathing during every third stroke forcing yourself to discover and improve your weaker side. If this is challenging & uncoordinated at first, hang in there - your clumsy side will improve with practice.  Kick & Rotate (K&R) - Begin kicking on your side for 3-5 seconds, then rotate onto your belly and "catch up" to your leading arm with the other arm bringing both arms out in front of you. Pull with the arm that was leading as you roll onto your opposite side. Kick for 3-5 seconds on this side, catch up with the other arm, and immediately pull the previously leading arm and rotate to the opposite side. Over time, try to spend only 2 seconds at a time kicking on each side.
Description: 10min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to warm up  3x5min Hard RPE7 (10k) pace, 3min easy  11min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to cool down

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Fists drills w/ 10s rest between drills 4x300m Moderate-Hard, RPE7 w/ 60s rest between intervals 4x50m Fastest Average, RPE9 w/ 20s rest between sprints 8x25m Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Fists (F) - Swim with clenched fists and focus on rotating your shoulders and bending your working elbow during the catch, creating a pull that's nearly as powerful as one done with an open hand.  Fastest Average (FA) - Swim each repetition at a pace that is as fast as you can sustain for the entire set, while still being able to complete all the repetitions at the given RPE. Aim to maintain a consistent time for each repetition throughout the set. For example: If doing 4 x 100m freestyle, it would be better to swim 1:20, 1:21, 1:19, 1:20 (with an average pace of 1:20 per 100m) than to swim 1:18, 1:18, 1:20, 1:28 (averaging 1:21 per 100m).  Kicking (K) - Swim facedown using only your feet to propel you as you breathe just as you would during a freestyle stroke by rolling to the side and turning your head to catch some air. Focus on "pushing" your chest down while keeping your legs & hips up toward the surface as you practice balance & kicking technique.

TSS 82, IF 0.74, kJ(Cal) 594.  Description: Titiroa is 25.5 minutes of near-steady output comprised of 3x8.5-minute segments at 97% FTP each separated by 9.5-minute recoveries. Goals: Muscular endurance workouts like this one are about as close to a full-on 40km time trial as you're likely to get without actually doing a 40km time trial.Do your best to keep your cadence in that 85-95rpm range and try to refrain from leaving your aero position for more than the few seconds it takes to briefly stretch.
Description: Warm-Up 250m Easy, RPE4 2000m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 Cool-Down 250m Easy, RPE4

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Stroke Counting drills w/ 10s rest between drills 7x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals 5x100m Hard, RPE8 w/ 60s rest between intervals 8x25m Side Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Stroke Counting (SC) - Target your stroke efficiency by trying to reduce the number of strokes you use to reach the opposite side of the pool. Count your strokes on your first trip across, then try to reduce the number of strokes during the next 2-3 trips across the pool by taking longer, more powerful pulls, rotating more & gliding a bit longer.  Side Kicking (SK) - Swim on your side with your lower arm extended ahead of you and the upper arm resting against your side. Rest your head against your shoulder and look down, keeping your head completely submerged while using only your kick to propel yourself. Rotate your head slightly when inhaling and try to keep your hips & legs high throughout as you swim a full pool length before switching sides.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Back Sculling drills w/ 10s rest between drills 4x300m Moderate-Hard, RPE7 w/ 45s rest between intervals 4x50m Fastest Average, RPE9 w/ 20s rest between sprints 8x25m Kick & Rotate drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Back Sculling (BS) - Keep your arms at your sides and point your fingertips back toward your toes as you propel yourself with those familiar figure-eight movements without moving your hands any wider than shoulder width.  Fastest Average (FA) - Swim each repetition at a pace that is as fast as you can sustain for the entire set, while still being able to complete all the repetitions at the given RPE. Aim to maintain a consistent time for each repetition throughout the set. For example: If doing 4 x 100m freestyle, it would be better to swim 1:20, 1:21, 1:19, 1:20 (with an average pace of 1:20 per 100m) than to swim 1:18, 1:18, 1:20, 1:28 (averaging 1:21 per 100m).  Kick & Rotate (K&R) - Begin kicking on your side for 3-5 seconds, then rotate onto your belly and "catch up" to your leading arm with the other arm bringing both arms out in front of you. Pull with the arm that was leading as you roll onto your opposite side. Kick for 3-5 seconds on this side, catch up with the other arm, and immediately pull the previously leading arm and rotate to the opposite side. Over time, try to spend only 2 seconds at a time kicking on each side.

Use a minimal kick and do this with your head up or down.  Kick & Rotate (K&R) - Begin kicking on your side for 3-5 seconds, then rotate onto your belly and "catch up" to your leading arm with the other arm bringing both arms out in front of you. Pull with the arm that was leading as you roll onto your opposite side. Kick for 3-5 seconds on this side, catch up with the other arm, and immediately pull the previously leading arm and rotate to the opposite side. Over time, try to spend only 2 seconds at a time kicking on each side.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Sighting drills w/ 10s rest between drills 4x300m Moderate-Hard, RPE7 w/ 30s rest between intervals 4x50m Fastest Average, RPE9 w/ 20s rest between sprints 8x25m Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Sighting (S) - Instead of turning your head to the side to breathe, raise your head to look forward and "sight" a landmark toward which you'll swim. Breathe just as you would when turning to the side by inhaling before lowering your head, exhaling underwater, and learn to disrupt your stroke as minimally as possible in the process - eventually not at all.  Fastest Average (FA) - Swim each repetition at a pace that is as fast as you can sustain for the entire set, while still being able to complete all the repetitions at the given RPE. Aim to maintain a consistent time for each repetition throughout the set. For example: If doing 4 x 100m freestyle, it would be better to swim 1:20, 1:21, 1:19, 1:20 (with an average pace of 1:20 per 100m) than to swim 1:18, 1:18, 1:20, 1:28 (averaging 1:21 per 100m).  Kicking (K) - Swim facedown using only your feet to propel you as you breathe just as you would during a freestyle stroke by rolling to the side and turning your head to catch some air. Focus on "pushing" your chest down while keeping your legs & hips up toward the surface as you practice balance & kicking technique.

TSS 35, IF 0.53, kJ(Cal) 439.  Description: Vladeasa consists of 75 minutes of aerobic Endurance riding spent between 50-55% FTP. Goals: Aerobic Endurance workouts are aimed at improving your aerobic (oxygen-reliant) power producing capabilities in a low-stress manner. By riding for increasingly longer periods of time, your endurance muscle fibers become more efficient at utilizing oxygen and sparing sugar stores for more intense efforts.Additionally, Endurance workouts like this can improve oxygen delivery, increase muscular resistance to fatigue, improve integrity of connective tissue, and even help you improve your on-bike nutrition strategies.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Single-Arm drills w/ 10s rest between drills 5x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Single-Arm (SA) - Restrict yourself to one arm?s use for an entire pool length by keeping the opposite arm extended in front of you or at your side (advanced). If necessary, perform these drills using fins until you improve to the point that you can eliminate them. Try to use a moderate kick and normal body positioning, rolling into your working arm.

Description: 45min at an Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace.

TSS 12, IF 0.50, kJ(Cal) 166.  Description: The Recess rides range anywhere from 20-60 minutes in duration at roughly 50% FTP.Exhausted rides, also referred to as 'accumulation rides' are performed within 4-24 hours after your last key workout, race, or event - any other physically stressful ride works as well. These rides should leave you no more fatigued than you were before you hopped on your bike and there are no strict rules governing effort level as long as these feel recuperative and do not negatively impact your next key workout. Goals: The goals behind the Recess rides are rather wide-ranging and include adaptations such as increased resistance to muscle damage, enhanced neuromuscular coordination (i.e., improved pedal economy), increased fat metabolism, and even heat acclimation to name a few.

TSS 65, IF 0.80, kJ(Cal) 480.  Description: Tunnabora is 5x7-minute sets of decreasing then increasing watts between 88-94% FTP in 1-minute steps to allow cadence changes over the course of each form interval. Tunnabora also includes 3x2-minute Isolated Leg Training (ILT) steps within the warmup and recoveries between intervals are each 3 minutes long. Goals: Running the entire Sweet Spot wattage range, this workout simultaneously targets improvements in muscle endurance while also aiming to improve your pedal economy.You'll first start with some low wattage 1-leggers before moving onto the main set where each time the watts drop you'll be encouraged to increase your cadence. Then, when the watts start to climb again, you'll back off your spin just enough to maintain your form.Aim for a slow, controlled, single-leg spin during the ILT drills until you get to the point where you can spin above 85rpm without "knocking".During the leg speed intervals, start conservatively and escalate from there, keeping your focus on controlled speed - excellent form always!

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 4x25m Catch-Up drills w/ 10s rest between drills 6x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals 4x25m Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Catch-Up (CU) - Your lead arm will remain fully extended in front of you until your working arm completes a full stroke. The working arm now becomes the lead arm and remains fully extended in front of you until your working arm completes its full stroke and "catches up".  Kicking (K) - Swim facedown using only your feet to propel you as you breathe just as you would during a freestyle stroke by rolling to the side and turning your head to catch some air. Focus on "pushing" your chest down while keeping your legs & hips up toward the surface as you practice balance & kicking technique.

Quick walking breaks of a single minute can often keep the form high while costing very little in terms of aerobic fitness improvements.

Side Kicking (SK) - Swim on your side with your lower arm extended ahead of you and the upper arm resting against your side. TSS 74, IF 0.77, kJ(Cal) 598.  Description: In total, Lowe is 49 minutes of  Tempo/Sweet Spot work broken into 6 segments ranging from 7.5-8.5 minutes and 80-88% FTP with only short, 1.5-minute recoveries between each, and a 4.5-minute recovery halfway through. Goals: Tempo rides are the height of aerobic endurance and while rather uncomfortable, they aren't too stressful to keep you from spending pretty long durations at a productive work level that isn't too demanding in terms of recovery.This is where some long-course and Olympic-distance triathletes will spend ample time. This is also where time is spent during any long but aggressive road ride.Use portions or entireties of these intervals to work on riding in your aerodynamic position without sacrificing power.There is no scheduled rest during this workout, but brief, occasional backspins are encouraged if they're the difference between loss of form and a high-quality workout.        Try to do so with a cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 90rpm.

Description: 40min at an Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace.   Again, walk briefly when necessary and try to recognize that quick walking breaks of a single minute can often keep the form high while costing very little in terms of aerobic fitness improvements.
Description: Warm-Up 250m Easy, RPE4 1200m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 Cool-Down 250m Easy, RPE4

TSS 71, IF 0.84, kJ(Cal) 515.  Description: Steamboat +2 consists of 5x8-minute spin-ups between 88-94% FTP where leg speed is the focus. Each interval consists of 4 quick minutes, 3 quicker minutes, and 1 really quick minute at relatively low power output.2 minutes of recovery fall between intervals. Goals: The primary focus is Improved pedaling mechanics through the use of high-cadence efforts that cultivate a more efficient pedalstroke and allow better use of energy at all leg speeds.Higher cadences reduce the level of muscle stress and shift the energy demand more toward the aerobic system and away from the anaerobic, sugar-dependent system - many light pedalstrokes utilize less sugar than fewer hard pedalstrokes.Cadence can climb as high as a rider can sustain while exhibiting excellent control but should fall no lower than 90rpm. ILT cadence is a low as necessary to learn single-leg control with a goal cadence of 90rpm/leg for all durations as improvement takes place.

Description: 35min at an Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace.   Keep inserting 1-minute walking breaks as necessary but try to increase the steady run durations for a minute or 2 more this week, maybe 6 or 7 minutes each time.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 4x25m Fingertips & Thumbs drills w/ 10s rest between drills 7x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals 4x25m Kick & Rotate drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Fingertips & Thumbs (F&T) - During your normal freestyle stroke, keep your elbows high and lightly drag your fingertips across the water as your working arm recovers. Then, as your working hand exits the water on the release, lightly scrape your thumb against your thigh. These two actions can be done individually or combined.  Kick & Rotate (K&R) - Begin kicking on your side for 3-5 seconds, then rotate onto your belly and "catch up" to your leading arm with the other arm bringing both arms out in front of you. Pull with the arm that was leading as you roll onto your opposite side. Kick for 3-5 seconds on this side, catch up with the other arm, and immediately pull the previously leading arm and rotate to the opposite side. Over time, try to spend only 2 seconds at a time kicking on each side.

TSS 76, IF 0.71, kJ(Cal) 697.  Description: Holt Hill totals over 70 minutes of Tempo intervals spent between 70-80% FTP with intermittent, 3-minute recoveries.  Goals: The goal here is to continue building aerobic power while refining your climbing technique and familiarizing your body with the different stresses of climbing.Additionally, there's focus on turning the pedals with higher than usual force while still remaining relaxed.You'll also target particular quadrants of the pedalstroke in order to keep power on the pedals more effectively throughout the entire 360-degree pedalstroke by focusing on lifting upward and kicking foward.These intervals are meant to simulate climbs, so try to use cadences that most closely mimic ones you'll use on grades you're likely to encounter in your events or rides.Recommended cadence is above 75rpm when seated and above 65rpm when standing.

Description: 40min at an Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace.   Shoot for those slightly longer stretches this week but keep your form at the top of your list of priorities. Much like swimming, small improvements in your technique can go a long way toward making you faster (and less prone to injury).

TSS 34, IF 0.67, kJ(Cal) 331.  Description: Carter is 45 minutes of aerobic Endurance riding where you'll spend 30 continuous minutes between 65-75% FTP. Goals: Aerobic Endurance training is primarily about cultivating a deeper aerobic base and includes all of the physiological adaptations inherent in long, steady, low-intensity riding like improved fat metabolism, better oxygen delivery and glycogen conservation, lower HR at higher power outputs, improved work efficiency, etc.As time-constrained as most riding schedules are though, shorter Endurance rides done consistently have to make up for some of the time restrictions faced by most riders.Try to keep your spin above 85rpm range and spend ample time adapting to and modifying your position on the bike while the workload is relatively gentle.
Description: 35min at an Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace.   Feel free to extend the occasional walking break up to 2 minutes in the interest of recharging your mind and body this week.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Bilateral Breathing drills w/ 10s rest between drills 6x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE 5 10s rest between intervals Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Bilateral Breathing (BB) - Swim your regular freestyle stroke while breathing during every third stroke forcing yourself to discover and improve your weaker side. If this is challenging & uncoordinated at first, hang in there - your clumsy side will improve with practice.

TSS 28, IF 0.53, kJ(Cal) 354.  Description: Bald -2 is 60 minutes of aerobic Recovery riding spent between 50-55% FTP. Goals: Recovery rides can be used to further your aerobic base by riding very easily when your muscles are already pretty cooked.When recovery is the goal, keep the ride as short as necessary and pull the plug well inside of an hour if effort this low is wearing you down and postponing your recovery.Spin lightly and reasonably quickly, preferably above 85rpm.
Description: Warm-Up 200m Easy, RPE4 1200m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 Cool-Down 200m Easy, RPE4

TSS 66, IF 0.81, kJ(Cal) 435.  Description: Capricorn consists of 2 x 4 minute 45 second intervals and 2 x 5-minute intervals at 106% FTP that alternates starting with the slightly shorter interval followed by a longer one etc.   Each full interval is separated by a 8 minute and 45 second  recovery valley. Goals: A noticeable drop from typical VO2 Max power allows far greater time at a potentially very productive power output well above your current sustainable power.The intent is to build greater muscular endurance and mental toughness by working at a high percentage of your maximal oxygen uptake with repeats that thoroughly stress your aerobic system. Shoot for a cadence above 85rpm, above 95rpm if possible.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Bilateral Breathing drills w/ 10s rest between drills 6x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals 5x100m Hard, RPE8 w/ 60s rest between intervals 8x25m Kick & Rotate drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Bilateral Breathing (BB) - Swim your regular freestyle stroke while breathing during every third stroke forcing yourself to discover and improve your weaker side. If this is challenging & uncoordinated at first, hang in there - your clumsy side will improve with practice.  Kick & Rotate (K&R) - Begin kicking on your side for 3-5 seconds, then rotate onto your belly and "catch up" to your leading arm with the other arm bringing both arms out in front of you. Pull with the arm that was leading as you roll onto your opposite side. Kick for 3-5 seconds on this side, catch up with the other arm, and immediately pull the previously leading arm and rotate to the opposite side. Over time, try to spend only 2 seconds at a time kicking on each side.
Description: 10min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to warm up  3x5min Hard RPE7 (10k) pace, 3min easy  11min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to cool down

TSS 79, IF 0.89, kJ(Cal) 539.  Description: Scylla is 3x12-minute over-under intervals alternating between 2 minutes at 97% FTP and 1 minute at 103% FTP with 6-minute recoveries between intervals. Goals: The primary objective of over-unders is to increase your ability to tolerate and utilize the byproducts that accompany riding above your FTP, all while maintaining a reasonably high power output. In doing so, you?ll develop the ability to handle changes in pace while doing quite a lot of work. The over-segments or 'surges' flood the muscle with metabolic byproducts bringing with them the desire to ease up - don't. Hold that burn, then back off only slightly during the under-segments or 'valleys' in order to work through the burn and effectively train your muscles to process the lactic buildup while still going pretty hard.These intervals can increase how long you can work above FTP as well as how quickly you can reprocess the lactate buildup that can lead to muscle acidity. Equally as important, the short efforts above your current FTP can actually lead to increases in FTP.Try to keep your cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 95rpm.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Fists drills w/ 10s rest between drills 4x300m Moderate-Hard, RPE7 w/ 60s rest between intervals 4x50m Fastest Average, RPE9 w/ 20s rest between sprints 8x25m Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Fists (F) - Swim with clenched fists and focus on rotating your shoulders and bending your working elbow during the catch, creating a pull that's nearly as powerful as one done with an open hand.  Fastest Average (FA) - Swim each repetition at a pace that is as fast as you can sustain for the entire set, while still being able to complete all the repetitions at the given RPE. Aim to maintain a consistent time for each repetition throughout the set. For example: If doing 4 x 100m freestyle, it would be better to swim 1:20, 1:21, 1:19, 1:20 (with an average pace of 1:20 per 100m) than to swim 1:18, 1:18, 1:20, 1:28 (averaging 1:21 per 100m).  Kicking (K) - Swim facedown using only your feet to propel you as you breathe just as you would during a freestyle stroke by rolling to the side and turning your head to catch some air. Focus on "pushing" your chest down while keeping your legs & hips up toward the surface as you practice balance & kicking technique.
Description: 10min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to warm up  20min Comfortably Hard RPE6 (Half Marathon) pace 10min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to cool down

TSS 110, IF 0.86, kJ(Cal) 748.  Description: Kaweah is 5x10-minute intervals between 96-99% FTP with 5-minute recoveries between intervals.  Goals: Kaweah's primary goal is quite simply to improve muscular endurance while adapting to an increasingly aerodynamic position.Additionally, and equally as important, riders can learn to tolerate the mental discomfort inherent in maintaining high levels of Functional Threshold Power.By spending time very close to lactate threshold while remaining just below it, riders can improve their sustainable power and muscle endurance by teaching their muscles to process high levels of lactate and tolerate the discomfort of acidic buildup without slowing down. Extra emphasis is placed on spending longer periods of time in an increasingly aero position striving to find that balance between aerodynamics and power output.Cadence should fall in the 85-95rpm range but steady stress and target watts are more important than leg speed so prioritize accordingly and don't worry if your cadence falls outside of this range from time to time.
Description: Warm-Up 250m Easy, RPE4 2000m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 Cool-Down 250m Easy, RPE4
Description: 90min at an Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace.

Each full interval is separated by a 5 and a half minute recovery valley. Goals: A noticeable drop from typical VO2 Max power allows far greater time at a potentially very productive power output well above your current sustainable power.The intent is to build greater muscular endurance and mental toughness by working at a high percentage of your maximal oxygen uptake with repeats that thoroughly stress your aerobic system. Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Stroke Counting drills w/ 10s rest between drills 7x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals 5x100m Hard, RPE8 w/ 60s rest between intervals 8x25m Side Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Stroke Counting (SC) - Target your stroke efficiency by trying to reduce the number of strokes you use to reach the opposite side of the pool. Count your strokes on your first trip across, then try to reduce the number of strokes during the next 2-3 trips across the pool by taking longer, more powerful pulls, rotating more & gliding a bit longer.  Side Kicking (SK) - Swim on your side with your lower arm extended ahead of you and the upper arm resting against your side. Rest your head against your shoulder and look down, keeping your head completely submerged while using only your kick to propel yourself. Rotate your head slightly when inhaling and try to keep your hips & legs high throughout as you swim a full pool length before switching sides.
Description: 10min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to warm up  4x5min Hard RPE7 (10k) pace, 3min easy  13min Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace to cool down

TSS 99, IF 0.89, kJ(Cal) 618.  Description: Dana -1 tones things down a bit but still offers 4x9-minute intervals where 2 minutes at 95% FTP alternate with a single minute way up at 120%.Recoveries between intervals are 6 minutes long. Goals: Over-unders are hard when they only push you slightly above FTP, but these brutes will hold you to an even higher performance standard and increase your ability to ride well above FTP before settling back into an effort level only slightly below your FTP. Work of this nature will challenge your highest-end aerobic power, your muscular endurance, your aerobic base, and your ability to truly suffer - all very valuable to the performance-conscious bike racer. Try to keep your spin above 90rpm during the under-segments and above 100rpm during the over-segments.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Back Sculling drills w/ 10s rest between drills 4x300m Moderate-Hard, RPE7 w/ 45s rest between intervals 4x50m Fastest Average, RPE9 w/ 20s rest between sprints 8x25m Kick & Rotate drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Back Sculling (BS) - Keep your arms at your sides and point your fingertips back toward your toes as you propel yourself with those familiar figure-eight movements without moving your hands any wider than shoulder width.  Fastest Average (FA) - Swim each repetition at a pace that is as fast as you can sustain for the entire set, while still being able to complete all the repetitions at the given RPE. Aim to maintain a consistent time for each repetition throughout the set. For example: If doing 4 x 100m freestyle, it would be better to swim 1:20, 1:21, 1:19, 1:20 (with an average pace of 1:20 per 100m) than to swim 1:18, 1:18, 1:20, 1:28 (averaging 1:21 per 100m).  Kick & Rotate (K&R) - Begin kicking on your side for 3-5 seconds, then rotate onto your belly and "catch up" to your leading arm with the other arm bringing both arms out in front of you. Pull with the arm that was leading as you roll onto your opposite side. Over time, try to spend only 2 seconds at a time kicking on each side.

Goals: The primary objective here is improvements in muscular endurance accomplished by growing your ability to repeat efforts at near-FTP wattages.By working close to but below your FTP, you not only accumulate a lot of time at a meaningful level of stress, but recovery from slightly sub-threshold effort comes much more readily than recovery following work done above your FTP.Try to hold your spin above 85rpm and even as high as 105rpm depending on your sustainable power and cadence goals.

Goals: The primary aim of Sweet Spot work is to improve your ability to resist fatigue at reasonably high power outputs over substantial lengths of time.In other words, workouts like this are all about increasing your muscular endurance.In doing so, you can improve your ability to climb faster and longer, face stiff winds without fading and time trial more consistently over the entire course of an event. Aim for a spin above 85rpm, preferably above 95rpm.

Description: 35min at an Easy RPE4 (Endurance) pace.   Feel free to insert 1 minute of walking whenever necessary as you try to run more continuously from week to week and emphasize quality over quantity at this early point in your running. Try to run for 5 minutes between walking breaks if possible.

TSS 25, IF 0.50, kJ(Cal) 332.  Description: The Recess rides range anywhere from 20-60 minutes in duration at roughly 50% FTP.Exhausted rides, also referred to as "accumulation rides" are performed within 4-24 hours after your last key workout, race/event, or any other similar, stressful ride. These rides should leave you no more fatigued than you were before you hopped on your bike and there are no strict rules governing effort level as long as these feel recuperative and do not negatively impact your next key workout. Goals: The goals behind the Recess rides are rather wide-ranging and include adaptations such as increased resistance to muscle damage, enhanced neuromuscular coordination (i.e. improved pedal economy), increased fat metabolism, and heat acclimation to name a few.

Goals: Recovery rides can be used to further your aerobic base by riding very easily when your muscles are already pretty cooked. When recovery is the goal, keep the ride as short as necessary and pull the plug well inside of an hour if effort this low is wearing you down and postponing your recovery.Spin lightly and reasonably quickly, preferably above 85rpm.

TSS 96, IF 0.80, kJ(Cal) 654.  Description: Elbow consists of 4x8.5-minute segments ranging from 99-100% FTP, each separated by 8.5-minute recoveries. Goals: The goal here is to elevate your strength endurance by learning to ride for longer and longer durations at your current FTP. As your body grows accustomed to a certain level of stress, your ability to withstand that stress for longer periods of time improves. Before you know it, your muscles don't load up like they used to, your mind isn't completely consumed with the desire to ease up, and your so-called "hour power" gravitates toward truer hour-power.The recommended cadence range is wide, 85-105 rpm, and accommodates many styles of riding. Riders wishing to focus more on improvements in strength endurance will spin toward the lower end of this cadence range while riders looking to foster a quicker spin will ride toward the upper end of the range.Or mix and match from interval to interval, just do your best to keep the power output smooth and consistent over the course of these 4 intervals.

TSS 91, IF 0.85, kJ(Cal) 626.  Description: Hayachin is made up of 2 sets of 2x7.5-minute intervals with 1x7.5-minute interval on it's own, with each consisting of 90-second steps up from 93-107% FTP and then back down again. Recovery between intervals is a brief 60 seconds while the recoveries between sets of stepping intervals are each 5 minutes long. Goals: This threshold workout targets both muscular endurance as well as lactate tolerance which takes place at both the muscular level as well as the psychological level.While your muscles will gradually adapt to increasing levels of lactate and acidic accumulation and improve their ability to shuttle these metabolic byproducts more effectively, your mind will grow accustomed to tolerating these increasing levels of discomfort, all to the same end - riding harder for longer.The true challenge with these little bad boys comes as you step back down without letting your cadence plummet - spin steadily for the whole interval!Try to keep your spin in the 85-95rpm range, higher if you can do it without elevating your HR to an unsustainable level.

TSS 87, IF 0.93, kJ(Cal) 512.  Description: Bowser is 5x5-minute intervals between 112-115% FTP with 4-minute recoveries between intervals. Goals: No two ways about it, these are hard. Holding power above FTP is, by its nature, short-term due to the fact that it's a tough place to reside.But by working at mildly suprathreshold power outputs, ones that take place at high percentages of your VO2max, you can push your FTP higher while gaining some meaningful aerobic adaptation in the process.On top of that, intervals that induce and sustain high levels of lactate work well toward lifting your physical (and mental) lactate tolerance.Strive for a cadence above 85rpm, preferably one above 95rpm.

TSS 41, IF 0.52, kJ(Cal) 517.  Description: Stickney consists of nearly 90 minutes of aerobic Endurance riding spent between 50-60% FTP. Goals: Aerobic Endurance workouts are aimed at improving your aerobic (oxygen-reliant) power producing capabilities in a low-stress manner. By riding for increasingly longer periods of time, your endurance muscle fibers become more efficient at utilizing oxygen and sparing sugar stores for more intense efforts.Additionally, Endurance workouts like this can improve oxygen delivery, increase muscular resistance to fatigue, improve integrity of connective tissue, and even help you improve your on-bike nutrition strategies.

Description: Warm-Up 200m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Fingertips & Thumbs drills w/ 10s rest between drills 4x200m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 45s rest between intervals 8x25m Side Kicking drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 200m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Fingertips & Thumbs (F&T) - During your normal freestyle stroke, keep your elbows high and lightly drag your fingertips across the water as your working arm recovers. Then, as your working hand exits the water on the release, lightly scrape your thumb against your thigh. These two actions can be done individually or combined.  Side Kicking (SK) - Swim on your side with your lower arm extended ahead of you and the upper arm resting against your side. Rest your head against your shoulder and look down, keeping your head completely submerged while using only your kick to propel yourself. Rotate your head slightly when inhaling and try to keep your hips & legs high throughout as you swim a full pool length before switching sides.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Half Catch-Up drills w/ 10s rest between drills 5x100m Easy-Moderate, RPE5 w/ 5s rest between intervals Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Half Catch-Up (HCU) - This abbreviated catch-up will bring the full catch-up drill closer to your actual freestyle stroke. Instead of waiting until your working arm reaches your lead arm, start pulling with your lead hand a little earlier when your working hand is even with your lead arm's elbow.

Manage that burn, then gradually back off slightly during the valleys in order to "work through the burn" and effectively train your muscles to process the buildup while your mind learns to compartmentalize the discomfort.Try to keep your cadence above 85rpm.

If necessary, perform these drills using fins until you improve to the point that you can eliminate them. Try to use a moderate kick and normal body positioning, rolling into your working arm. Try to do so with a cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 90rpm.There is no scheduled rest during this workout, but brief, occasional backspins are encouraged if they're the difference between loss of form and a high-quality workout.

Breathe just as you would when turning to the side by inhaling before lowering your head, exhaling underwater, and learn to disrupt your stroke as minimally as possible in the process - eventually not at all.

Description: Warm-Up 300m Easy, RPE4 8x25m Half Catch-Up drills w/ 10s rest between drills 5x250m Moderate-Hard, RPE7 w/ 45s rest between intervals 5x50m Fastest Average, RPE9 w/ 20s rest between sprints 8x25m Kick & Rotate drills w/ 15s rest between drills Cool-Down 300m Easy, RPE4  Drill Instructions Half Catch-Up (HCU) - This abbreviated catch-up will bring the full catch-up drill closer to your actual freestyle stroke. Instead of waiting until your working arm reaches your lead arm, start pulling with your lead hand a little earlier when your working hand is even with your lead arm's elbow.  Fastest Average (FA) - Swim each repetition at a pace that is as fast as you can sustain for the entire set, while still being able to complete all the repetitions at the given RPE. Aim to maintain a consistent time for each repetition throughout the set. For example: If doing 4 x 100m freestyle, it would be better to swim 1:20, 1:21, 1:19, 1:20 (with an average pace of 1:20 per 100m) than to swim 1:18, 1:18, 1:20, 1:28 (averaging 1:21 per 100m).  Kick & Rotate (K&R) - Begin kicking on your side for 3-5 seconds, then rotate onto your belly and "catch up" to your leading arm with the other arm bringing both arms out in front of you. Pull with the arm that was leading as you roll onto your opposite side. Kick for 3-5 seconds on this side, catch up with the other arm, and immediately pull the previously leading arm and rotate to the opposite side. Over time, try to spend only 2 seconds at a time kicking on each side.

Make the same figure-eight patterns with both hands to propel yourself forward, effectively repeating the center of the stroke.  Fastest Average (FA) - Swim each repetition at a pace that is as fast as you can sustain for the entire set, while still being able to complete all the repetitions at the given RPE. Aim to maintain a consistent time for each repetition throughout the set. Side Kicking (SK) - Swim on your side with your lower arm extended ahead of you and the upper arm resting against your side. Rest your head against your shoulder and look down, keeping your head completely submerged while using only your kick to propel yourself. Rotate your head slightly when inhaling and try to keep your hips & legs high throughout as you swim a full pool length before switching sides.

TSS 96, IF 0.69, kJ(Cal) 907.  Description: Perkins is a highly varied 2 hours of aerobic Endurance riding ranging from 50-80% FTP. Goals: The work here targets improvements in aerobic endurance/aerobic base by asking you to work at a moderate intensities well below your FTP. By sustaining this effort level for fairly long and sometimes quite long, minimally interrupted periods of time, your body will improve its aerobic capabilities thereby improving your level of power output at low to moderate levels of exertion. Greater aerobic capbilities means you can work harder before dipping very far into your precious sugar stores thereby preserving those stores for the higher-intensity efforts. This also means you'll require less food/carbohydrate to fuel your effort during your events.Try to maintain a cadence between 85-95rpm.

TSS 46, IF 0.68, kJ(Cal) 446.  Description: Beech is 60 minutes of aerobic Endurance riding spent somewhere between 65-75% FTP. Goals: Endurance workouts are aimed at improving your aerobic power production capabilities in a steady, low-stress manner. By training your endurance muscle fibers to better utilize oxygen to metabolize fat as fuel, your muscles can spare sugar stores for more intense efforts.This long/slow approach to fostering aerobic capabilities is the lighter, kinder alternative to high-intensity repeats, but both approaches target many of the same  adaptations.Try to keep your spin above 85rpm, preferably above 95rpm.

TSS 59, IF 0.89, kJ(Cal) 373.  Description: Adams is reduced-load assortment of suprathreshold efforts consisting of 7 minutes at 105% FTP, 5 minutes at 108% FTP, 3 minutes at 115% FTP and 2 minutes at 121% FTP.3-minute recoveries separate all intervals. Goals: The aim here is to keep increasing the demand placed on your metabolic capabilities with the intent of furthering your sustainable, repeatable high-end power output.Shoot for a cadence above 85rpm on the longer intervals, ideally above 95rpm on the shorter ones.

TSS 65, IF 0.72, kJ(Cal) 585.  Description: White is 60 continuous minutes of Tempo work broken into 6x10-minute segments ranging from 70-80% FTP. Goals: Tempo rides are the height of aerobic endurance and while rather uncomfortable, they aren't too stressful to keep you from spending pretty long durations at a productive work level that isn't too demanding in terms of recovery.This is where many ultra-distance triathletes will live for hours at a time. This is also where a lot of time is spent during any long road ride.Use portions or entireties of these intervals to work on riding in your aerodynamic position without sacrificing power.There is no scheduled rest during this workout, but brief, occasional backspins are encouraged if they're the difference between loss of form and a high-quality workout.Try to do so with a cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 90rpm.

TSS 83, IF 0.74, kJ(Cal) 728.  Description: Echo is 80 total minutes of Tempo work broken into 8x10-minute segments ranging from 70-80% FTP. Goals: Tempo rides are the height of aerobic endurance and while rather uncomfortable, they aren't too stressful to keep you from spending pretty long durations at a productive work level that isn't too demanding in terms of recovery.This is where many ultra-distance triathletes will live for hours at a time. This is also where a lot of time is spent during any long road ride. If applicable, use portions or entireties of these intervals to work on riding in your aerodynamic position without sacrificing power.There is no scheduled rest during this workout, but brief, occasional backpedal breaks are encouraged if they're the difference between loss of form and a high-quality workout.Try to do so with a cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 90rpm.

TSS 79, IF 0.80, kJ(Cal) 616.  Description: Piute is a progression workout where each 10% step lasts 12 minute and starts at 60% FTP and then progresses the workload up to 100% FTP during the final step.  Goals: The muscular endurance intervals in this particular sequence of workouts - consisting of Rendezvous, Piute, Ragged & Tower - grow in duration each workout via slightly longer steps. Progression Workouts ever so gradually increase the power requirement until you are working at your Functional Threshold Power, all in an effort to raise that maximum steady-state pace, your ability to withstand increasing physical discomfort, and your time to exhaustion.Do your best to keep your cadence in that 85-95rpm range with each upward step in power output, and spend greater lengths of time in your aero position even as the power requirements grow.

This is also where most time is spent during any long road ride.Use portions or entireties of these intervals to work on riding in your aerodynamic position without sacrificing power.There is no scheduled rest during this workout, but brief, occasional backspins are encouraged if they're the difference between loss of form and a high-quality workout.Try to do so with a cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 90rpm.

TSS 44, IF 0.66, kJ(Cal) 438.  Description: Bald is 60 minutes of aerobic Endurance riding spent between 65-70% FTP.Optional form drills include some Endurance Spinning, some Single-Leg Focus & some Kick & Pull form work. Goals: Aerobic Endurance workouts are aimed at improving your aerobic (i.e. non-sugar) power-producing capabilities in a low-stress manner.By riding for increasingly longer periods of time, your endurance muscle fibers become more efficient at utilizing oxygen and sparing sugar stores for more intense efforts.Also, some of the shorter Endurance workouts are aimed primarily at keeping you on the bike as a form of active recovery during recovery days or weeks.

Goals: Occasionally, you're simply too tired to work out but could very well benefit from a short, easy recovery ride, and that's exactly what this workout isDans can also be used as an extended warm-up prior to each workout's more specific warm-up, or it can simply be used to tack on some easy mileage at the end of another workout.

TSS 39, IF 0.63, kJ(Cal) 412.  Description: Pettit is an hour of aerobic Endurance work spent between 60-70% FTP.Optional form drills include form sprints, speed-endurance intervals, and quadrant drills. Goals: Aerobic Endurance workouts are aimed at improving your aerobic-power-producing capabilities in a low stress manner.By riding for increasingly longer periods of time, your endurance muscle fibers become more efficient at utilizing fat for fuel and sparing sugar stores for more intense efforts.Additionally, Endurance workouts like this can improve oxygen delivery & utilization, increase muscular resistance to fatigue, improve integrity of connective tissue, and even help you improve your on-bike nutrition strategies.Regarding the form drills, Pettit targets improvements in pedaling mechanics, leg speed, and sprinting form.

Description: Pettit is an hour of aerobic Endurance work spent between 60-70% FTP.Optional form drills include form sprints, speed-endurance intervals, and quadrant drills. Goals: Aerobic Endurance workouts are aimed at improving your aerobic-power-producing capabilities in a low stress manner.By riding for increasingly longer periods of time, your endurance muscle fibers become more efficient at utilizing fat for fuel and sparing sugar stores for more intense efforts.Additionally, Endurance workouts like this can improve oxygen delivery & utilization, increase muscular resistance to fatigue, improve integrity of connective tissue, and even help you improve your on-bike nutrition strategies.Regarding the form drills, Pettit targets improvements in pedaling mechanics, leg speed, and sprinting form.

TSS 50, IF 0.70, kJ(Cal) 458.  Description: Mont Albert is 1 hour of Tempo intervals spent between 70-80% FTP with intermittent, 1 to 3-minute recoveries at 55% FTP.  Goals: Outside of the climbing goals of refining your climbing technique & familiarizing your body with the somewhat different stress of climbing, the additional aim here is to continue building aerobic power.Try to employ realistic climbing cadences if you're simulating hill climbs. Ideally, seated climbing should be done above 80rpm and try to stay above 70rpm when standing. But if you know the particular demands of the climbs you'll face, feel free to use suitable cadences specific to your needs.

TSS 64, IF 0.71, kJ(Cal) 581.  Description: Mont Gosford is 1 hour and 15 minutes of Tempo intervals spent between 70-80% FTP with intermittent, 2 to 3-minute recoveries at 55% FTP.  Goals: Outside of the climbing goals of refining your climbing technique & familiarizing your body with the somewhat different stress of climbing, the additional aim here is to continue building aerobic power.Try to employ realistic climbing cadences if you're simulating hill climbs. Ideally, seated climbing should be done above 80rpm and try to stay above 70rpm when standing. But if you know the particular demands of the climbs you'll face, feel free to use suitable cadences specific to your needs.

TSS 69, IF 0.83, kJ(Cal) 460.  Description: Mansfield is 4x5-minute intervals at 105% FTP with 5-minute recoveries following each interval.  Goals: The primary goal here is to improve muscular endurance while climbing and to reinforce high levels of muscle control during slower, high-force efforts.Additionally, the optional drills target improvements in your ability to not only transition in and out of the saddle smoothly, but to do so while limiting fluctuations in power output.Finally, you'll have the option to work on refining your body positioning when standing as well as improve your pedaling technique when riding out of the saddle.

TSS 59, IF 0.77, kJ(Cal) 474.  Description: Slide Mountain is 3x10-minute intervals at 85% FTP with 4-minute recoveries between intervals. Goals: The primary aim of Tempo work is to improve your ability to resist fatigue at reasonably high power outputs over substantial lengths of time.In other words, workouts like this are all about increasing your muscular endurance.In doing so, you can improve your ability to climb faster and longer, face stiff winds without fading, and time trial more consistently over the entire course of an event. Additionally, you have the option of targeting specific cadences with the aim of increasing your speed endurance as well as improving your ability to pedal properly during slower spins.Cadence recommendations vary depending on the current drill, but riders choosing to omit the drills should aim for a spin between 85-95rpm.

TSS 19, IF 0.62, kJ(Cal) 202.  Description: Taku is a short, aerobic Endurance workout consisting of 30 minutes spent between 60-70% FTP.Taku can serve as a light workout on its own, it can be paired with a short, intense interval session, beforehand or afterward, or it can be used to get you on the bike between hard workouts in order to spin out some heaviness. Goals: Endurance workouts are aimed at improving your aerobic power production capabilities in a steady, low-stress manner. By training your endurance muscle fibers to better utilize oxygen to metabolize fat as fuel, your muscles can spare sugar stores for more intense efforts.

TSS 101, IF 0.76, kJ(Cal) 874.  Description: Virginia +2 consists of 1.75 hours of Tempo work spent between 75-80% FTP. Goals: Tempo intervals are primarily aimed at increasing aerobic capabilities via reasonably long, mildly more intense work than your typical long-duration, minimally-changing aerobic endurance workouts.The workloads are low enough that you can spend a lot of time at this intensity without requiring disproportionately long recovery periods like you might face when attempting high amounts of work performed closer to your FTP. But the intensity is high enough to stimulate greater adaptation than you?re likely to see when noodling along at Endurance power for durations as short as these.      Try to keep your spin above 85rpm, preferably closer to 95rpm for most athletes.

TSS 98, IF 0.88, kJ(Cal) 659.  Description: Carpathian Peak +1 is 3x15-minute over-under intervals with 1-minute valleys at 95% FTP and 2-minute peaks that ascend to 105% FTP.Recoveries between intervals are 7 minutes long. Goals: The primary objective of over-unders is to increase your ability to tolerate and utilize the byproducts that accompany riding above your FTP, all while maintaining a reasonably high power output. In doing so, you'll develop the ability to handle changes in pace while doing quite a lot of work. The over-segments or 'surges' flood the muscle with metabolic byproducts bringing with them the desire to ease up - don't. Hold that burn, then back off only slightly during the under-segments or 'valleys' in order to work through the burn and effectively train your muscles to process the lactic buildup while still going pretty hard.These intervals can increase how long you can work above FTP as well as how quickly you can reprocess the metabolic buildup that can limit your capabilities. Equally as important, the short efforts above your current FTP can actually lead to increases in FTP.Try to keep your cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 95rpm.

TSS 64, IF 0.80, kJ(Cal) 495.  Description: Rendezvous is 50 minutes of continuous progression from 60% FTP all the way up to 100% FTP via 10% increases every 10 minutes.  Goals: Progression workouts ever so gradually increase the power requirement until you are working at your highest sustainable power, all in an effort to raise your muscular endurance, grow your ability to withstand increasing physical discomfort and your increase your time to exhaustion.By gradually raising your power and along with it your fatigue, acidic accumulation and lactate levels, the continually increasing power demand will serve as the stimulus for subsequent adaptation. Translation: this workout + proper recovery = more power for longer spans of time.Do your best to keep your cadence in that 85-95rpm range with each upward step in power output, and spend greater lengths of time in your aero position if your discipline requires riding low.

TSS 100, IF 0.75, kJ(Cal) 864.  Description: Gibraltar -2 is 105 minutes of continuous riding where you'll spend 90 minutes between 75-80% FTP. Goals: Tempo rides are the height of aerobic endurance and while rather uncomfortable, they aren't too stressful to keep you from spending pretty long durations at a productive work level that isn't too demanding in terms of recovery.This is where conservative long-course and aggressive ultra-distance triathletes will live for hours at a time. This is also where a lot of time is spent during any long road ride.Use portions or entireties of these intervals to work on riding in your aerodynamic position without sacrificing power. Try to do so with a cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 90rpm.      There is no scheduled rest during this workout, but brief, occasional backspins are encouraged if they're the difference between loss of form and a high-quality workout.

TSS 63, IF 0.80, kJ(Cal) 430.  Description: Sanford +1 is 6x3-minute intervals at 105% FTP with 4-minute recoveries between intervals. Goals: Not going to sugarcoat things: these are rough. At least in terms of suffering. But that's a big part of what makes working slightly above FTP so effective. The goal of slightly suprathreshold workouts is to extend high power over greater durations. By riding only slightly above your current FTP, you'll become accustomed - both physically and mentally - to a workload just beyond your current sustainable reach.These intervals are also an ideal time to begin bridging the gap between power in an upright riding position and power in an aerodynamic position.Strive to keep your cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 95rpm.

TSS 79, IF 0.89, kJ(Cal) 529.  Description: Washington -2 is 4x7-minute intervals at 105% FTP including brief, 30-second recoveries in the middle of each interval and short, 2-minute recoveries between intervals. Goals: The goal of slightly suprathreshold workouts is to extend high power over greater durations. By riding only slightly above your current FTP, you'll become accustomed - both physically and mentally - to a workload just beyond your current sustainable reach. These intervals are also an ideal time to begin bridging the gap between power in an upright riding position and power in an aerodynamic position.Strive to keep your cadence above 85rpm, preferably above 95rpm.

TSS 78, IF 0.89, kJ(Cal) 509.  Description: Ainslie +3 is 7x4-minute intervals at 105% FTP with 2-minute recoveries after each.  Goals: The goal of supra-threshold workouts is to extend high power over greater durations, and each similar version of this particular type of suffering will include more or longer work intervals while the recovery valleys will often shrink from training phase to training phase.These intervals are also an ideal time to begin bridging the gap between power in an upright riding position and power in an aerodynamic position.Strive to keep your cadence above 90rpm, even closer to 100rpm if possible.

TSS 119, IF 0.89, kJ(Cal) 748.  Description: Warlow +2 is 5x9-minute over-under intervals alternating between 2 minutes pushed up to 98% FTP and 1 minute still at 115% FTP with 6-minute recoveries between intervals. Goals: This workout chiefly aims to develop your ability to handle changes in pace while maintaining a high level of work and enhance muscle endurance in the process.The surges flood the muscle with metabolic byproducts bringing with them the desire to ease up, don't. Hold that burn, then back off only slightly during the valleys in order to work through the burn and effectively train your muscles to process the buildup while still doing a lot of work.These intervals can increase your ability to better process lactate thereby increasing not only how long you can work above FTP but also increasing FTP itself.Try to keep your cadence in the 85-95rpm range whether enduring a valley or powering through a short surge.

TSS 64, IF 0.80, kJ(Cal) 426.  Description: Riscos is an assortment of suprathreshold efforts consisting of 2x5 minutes at 102% FTP, 4 minutes at 105% FTP, 2 minutes at 110% FTP and 2 minutes at 115% FTP. Recoveries between intervals are all 3 minutes long. Goals: When your fitness nears its peak, it's beneficial to combine numerous types of suprathreshold and VO2max intervals in a more race-realistic format. The aim here is to keep increasing the demand placed on your metabolic capabilities with the intent of furthering your sustainable, repeatable high-end power output.Shoot for a cadence above 85rpm on the longer intervals, ideally above 95rpm on the shorter ones.

TSS 66, IF 0.81, kJ(Cal) 429.  Description: Chilly is an assortment of suprathreshold efforts consisting of 2x5 minutes at 104% FTP, 4 minutes at 107% FTP, 2 minutes at 112% FTP and 2 minutes at 115% FTP. Recoveries between intervals are all 3 minutes long. Goals: When your fitness nears its peak, it's beneficial to combine numerous types of suprathreshold and VO2max intervals in a more race-realistic format. The aim here is to keep increasing the demand placed on your metabolic capabilities with the intent of furthering your sustainable, repeatable high-end power output.Shoot for a cadence above 85rpm on the longer intervals, ideally above 95rpm on the shorter ones.

TSS 68, IF 0.82, kJ(Cal) 432.  Description: Jungbong is an assortment of suprathreshold efforts consisting of 2x5 minutes at 105% FTP, 4 minutes at 108% FTP, 2 minutes at 115% FTP and 2 minutes at 121% FTP.Recoveries between intervals are all 3 minutes long. Goals: When your fitness nears its peak, it's beneficial to combine numerous types of suprathreshold and VO2max intervals in a more race-realistic format. The aim here is to keep increasing the demand placed on your metabolic capabilities with the intent of furthering your sustainable, repeatable high-end power output.Shoot for a cadence above 85rpm on the longer intervals, ideally above 95rpm on the shorter ones.

TSS 33, IF 0.81, kJ(Cal) 227.  Description: Marys is 2x6-minute intervals at 98% FTP with 5-minutes of recovery in between. Goals: The primary objective here is improvement in muscular endurance accomplished by growing your ability to repeat efforts at high percentages of your FTP.By working close to but below your FTP, you not only accumulate a lot of time at a meaningful level of stress, but recovery from slightly sub-threshold effort comes much more readily than recovery following work done at or just above your FTP.Try to hold your spin above 85rpm and even as high as 105rpm depending on your sustainable power and cadence goals.
